items.misconception: 3/7, by adding the numerators and the denominators separately.
items.why: Students apply the (correct) fraction multiplication rule to addition, treating the numerator and denominator as independent.

items.q: If you've flipped a fair coin and got heads 5 times in a row, what is the probability the next flip is heads?
items.correct: 1/2 — each flip is independent of the previous ones.
items.misconception: Less than 1/2, because the coin is 'due' to land on tails to balance the results.
items.random_wrong: 1 because the coin has clearly been biased by the previous flips.
items.why: The gambler's fallacy — humans seek patterns and think outcomes 'balance out' over short runs, but each independent flip has no memory.

items.q: Are 'independent events' and 'mutually exclusive events' the same?
items.correct: No — independent events do not affect each other's probability; mutually exclusive events cannot both happen.
items.misconception: Yes — both mean the events are unrelated.
items.random_wrong: Yes, but only when the probabilities are equal.
items.why: Students learn these terms close together and conflate them; in fact mutually exclusive non-zero events are never independent.
Correct answer: π. “0.5” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's 9. “11” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 36. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
